options.why: Kept the pronumeral before the number; the convention writes the number first.
Read the $\times$ as a $+$, adding instead of multiplying.
Turned the coefficient $6$ into a power instead of leaving it as a multiplier.
Left the number after the pronumeral; the convention puts the number first.
Wrote the $3$ as a power on $t$ instead of as a coefficient in front.
Interpreted the $\times$ as addition rather than multiplication.
Kept the coefficient $1$ visible; a coefficient of $1$ is hidden.
Hid the $1$ but also dropped the negative sign.
Dropped the pronumeral $y$, treating $-1 \times y$ as just $-1$.
Confused $w \times w$ with $w + w$, giving a coefficient of $2$ instead of a power.
Dropped the $\times$ but did not write the repeated factor as a power.
Wrote the pronumeral only once, ignoring the repeated multiplication.
Inverted the fraction, putting the divisor $3$ on top instead of underneath.
Read the $\div$ as $\times$ and wrote a product.
Interpreted the $\div$ as subtraction rather than division.

questions.question_text: Write $5g$ in full, showing the multiplication sign.

options.why: Read the coefficient as an addition instead of a multiplication.
Treated the $5$ as an index (repeated multiplication) rather than a coefficient.
Read $5g$ as $g$ to the power $5$, confusing a coefficient with an index.

questions.question_text: Write $x \times 7 \times x \times y$ using algebraic conventions.

options.why: Read $x \times x$ as $2x$ and multiplied it into the $7$, turning the power into a coefficient.
Put the power on $y$ instead of on the repeated factor $x$.
Did not write the number first; the coefficient belongs at the front.
